completionNotes: Completed as a submitted-query search pattern with explicit result-count feedback, clear-query recovery, related-pattern distinctions, source-backed evidence, and screenshot spot-check evidence.

completionEvidence.patternSpecificGuidance: Guidance names query entry, submitted search scope, result count, result-list placement, clear-query recovery, and no-results handling for searchable collections.
completionEvidence.relatedPatternDistinction: The entry separates basic search from command palette side-effect actions, search suggestions query formulation, and faceted search structured narrowing.
completionEvidence.liveDemoBehavior: The live demo uses a role=search form with a draft query, submit action, persistent submitted-query result count, clear action, and no-match recovery state.
completionEvidence.comparisonDecisionRules: The linked basic-search-vs-command-palette comparison gives concrete rules for content retrieval versus cross-product command execution and novice workflow visibility.
completionEvidence.duplicateAudit: npm run audit:completion compares normalized guidance fields across patterns and currently reports no duplicate field group blocking this completed pattern.
completionEvidence.manualSpotCheck: screenshots/patterns/basic-search.png was inspected after rendering and shows Good UI, Bad UI, Good UX, Bad UX, related links, comparison link, and source-backed guidance.

name: Basic search
problem: Users need to find matching content without browsing every category or page.
solution: Provide a labeled search field, run the query against relevant content, and show result count, matches, and recovery paths.
problemContext: The content set is larger than users can comfortably scan.
Users can describe what they need with words or identifiers.
selectionRules: Choose basic search when the task is content retrieval.
Show results and counts close to the search input.
Preserve the query while users inspect or refine results.
interactionContract: Typing or submitting a query changes the visible result set predictably.
The user can clear or revise the query.
Result labels explain what will open or happen.
implementationChecklist: Provide a visible label or accessible name.
Show result count and matching items.
Keep filters and sort behavior clear.
commonMisuses: Using placeholder text as the only label.
Returning no feedback while results update.
Making search change unrelated filters silently.
critiqueQuestions: Can users predict what content the search covers?
useWhen: Users know a word, name, or identifier.
The result set can be meaningfully ranked or filtered.
avoidWhen: The task is choosing a command with side effects.
The content set is tiny and easier to scan.
accessibility: The input has a stable accessible name.
Result count changes are communicated without excessive announcements.
keyboardBehavior: Enter submits when search is explicit.
Escape or a clear button can reset the query when provided.
failureModes: Searching the wrong scope without telling users.
Destroying the query after opening a result.
Showing irrelevant suggestions as if they are results.

sources.note: MOJ search guidance supports a labeled search field, submit action, hints, and no-results recovery for search tasks.
APG landmark guidance supports exposing search as a named search region.
GOV.UK patterns emphasize task-oriented search, filtering, and recovery states.

uiGuidance: Render a labeled search field, result count, matching rows, and clear action.
Keep result updates visually close to the query that caused them.
uxGuidance: Help users find known content without browsing every category.
Preserve query context while users inspect, refine, or recover from no results.

good: A clearly labeled search field with result count and results placed directly below.
Matched rows use calm highlighting and stable spacing so scanning stays easy.
bad: Search input hidden behind only an icon with no label.
Results jump, resize, or use heavy borders that make scanning feel like reading a form.

good: Users can type, revise, clear, and keep context while inspecting results.
No-results state offers query correction, clearing, or broader search.
bad: Opening a result destroys the query and makes returning impossible.

examples.notes: Shows a search field with an explicit search action and guidance for no-results recovery.
